oai_dc:dc.dc:title: First Breeding Groove-billed Toucanets at Emerald Forest Bird Gardens
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Jennings, Jerry
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This article originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 51 Number 3 (July 2024). The text below contains excerpts from the original article. For photographs, figures, advertisements, and the complete formatted version as originally published, please use the PDF link associated with this article. In November 2022, we acquired five DNA sexed pairs of Groove-billed Toucanets (Aulachorynchus sulcatus sulcatus) - the nominate form. There are three distinct subspecies of Groove Bills all found in Venezuela and Colombia. None have appeared in U.S. aviculture for several decades except for a couple of individual birds of the nominate race in the 1970s. The five pairs were set up in aviaries at Emerald Forest Bird Gardens, one pair to a flight. Three different flight types, varying in size from 8' x 12' to 10' x 18' to 12' x 24' were used. Each flight was equipped with a nest log made from a four foot length of palm tree trunk which had been hollowed out to a depth of two feet and a diameter of eight inches. On May 20, 2023, a pair in a 10' x18' foot flight began incubating three eggs which hatched on June 5, 2023. Three days later, these three babies were pulled for handrearing. They were kept in an Avey Incubator Chick Chalet brooder until five weeks of age, then moved to a small rearing cage in our nursery for weaning. The babies were exclusively fed Mazuri handfeeding formula and weaned onto a diet of Mazuri Toucan Pellets and mixed fruit which consisted of papaya, cantaloupe, honeydew, watermelon, bananas and blueberries. These babies were weaned by August 9, 2023. One week after these two Groove Bills hatched, a second pair of Groove Bills hatched four babies on June 14, 2023. They were housed in a smaller flight measuring 8' x 12' and were pulled for hand rearing at three days of age. They were moved to a weaning cage at five weeks of age and are now weaned. Much to our surprise and excitement, this second pair went back to nest a second time and hatched out three more babies on July 3, 2023. We intended leaving this second nest to be raised by their parents, but the unexpected Tropical Storm Hillary soaked the mother so she couldn't fly, necessitating she and her three babies be pulled. The babies were feathered so we put both the babies and the female into a 4' long weaning cage to which they quickly adjusted. The mother continued feeding her babies while we supplemented with handfeeding formula until we were sure she was feeding adequately and they are now weaned. It is interesting to note here that these parent reared babies grew much faster than handfed babies. We have fledged nine baby Groove Bills so far in our first breeding season with this species. All the adult Groove Bills we acquired were DNA sexed. Upon examination it seemed apparent that they were dimorphic as females had noticeably shorter beaks than males corroborated by the known DNA results. It is reassuring that this species has done so well shortly after appearing in American aviculture and this bodes well for their continued existence. Click the PDF link associated with this article to view the complete article as originally published in AFA Watchbird.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 51 No. 3 (2024): AFA Watchbird July 2024
